---
title: "Inferential Methods in R"
subtitle: "Statistical Analysis using R"
author: "Carlos Chávez"
format: 
    html:
      toc: true
      toc-float: true
      toc-expand: true
      toc-depth: 5
      number-sections: true
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

# Learning Objectives

1. Understand and apply different R functions for conducting common statistical tests.

2. Read and interpret output from statistical analysis.

3. Use R to create tables and figures of quantitative findings.

# Overview of functions and packages

Here is a list of all the packages that we will be using in this workshop. These packages contain different functions that we will be using to carry out analytical tasks. Functions require inputs (arguments) to process an output (result). 

```{r}
#| label: Packages
#| echo: true
#| message: false
library(tidyverse) # Contains the most commonly used packages from the tidyr suite.
library(knitr) # Contains the `kable()` function which is useful for creating nice tables
library(kableExtra) # Additional table styling and options for `kable`
library(mosaic) # Using `favstats` to get summary statistics and tables
library(modelsummary) # If we have time - contains functions like tidy() and augment() which extract regression results in a tidy format. Also useful when creating tables of multiple models.
```

We will be using the `penguins` data set that comes with R for this portion of the workshop. This is a data set that we commonly use across SDS courses to demonstrate and practice with a variety of R functions.

```{r}
#| label: Import Penguins Data
penguins <- palmerpenguins::penguins
```

::: {.callout-tip}
You can always view the data set in a separate window by clicking on the data set in your environment. 
:::

# Methods of Analyses

## T Tests

:::{.panel-tabset}


There are a few functions we can use to conduct 1 and 2 sample t-tests. As a brief refresher, t-tests are a family of statistical tests that often used to evaluate if an observed mean statistically significant.

### One Sample T-Test

For a one-sample t-test we might ask:

> Is the average body mass of penguins in 2008 different from the average body mass in 2007?

The code below produces a table of summary statistics (i.e., sample size, mean, median, standard deviation, etc.) of body mass by year.

Notice that we use formula notation for specifying the inputs of `favstats`. Much like a formula or equation we are specifying a left hand side and a right hand side of the equation, and then adding an argument for the data set we are using. 

```
Left Hand Side ~ Right Hand Side 
Response Variable ~ Explanatory Variable
```

```{r}
#| label: Summary statistics for body mass and year
#| echo: true

favstats(body_mass_g ~ year, data = penguins)

```

We can also create a box plot that will visualize these differences.

```{r}
#| label: Box plot for body mass and year

penguins |> # Specify the data set
  filter(
    year != 2009, # Filter rows where year is not equal to 2009 and 
    !is.na(body_mass_g)  # Filter rows where body_mass_g is not NA
    ) |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x = body_mass_g,
    fill = factor(year) # Make year a factor variable to group box plots
             )
         ) + 
  geom_boxplot() + # Create a box plot layer
  labs(
    title = "Body Mass of Penguins by Year", # Add a title to my plot
    x = "Body Mass (g)", # Rename x axis
    fill = "Year" # Rename legend title
    ) +
  theme_classic()

```

We can conduct a one sample t-test using the `t.test` function

In the `t.test` function there are three inputs.

a.  The first is a formula to specify that we are only considering a single variable, `body_mass_g`, which is on the right hand side of the equation.
b.  Second, we specify the data set so R knows where to look for the variable.
c.  And third, we specify a null value to conduct the hypothesis test.

```{r}
#| label: One Sample T-Test

# Create a version of the penguins data set that only contains data from 2008
penguins_008 <- penguins |> 
  filter(year == 2008) 

t.test(~ body_mass_g, data = penguins_008, mu = 4124.541)


```

1. What is the p-value? 0.0571

2. What is the 95% confidence interval? [4120.177, 4413.156]

3. What is the sample mean of body mass? 4124.541

4. What can we conclude about the average penguin body mass in 2008? The average body mass of penguins in 2008 is not all that different from 2007. 


### Two Sample T-Test

Keeping the same variable, `body_mass_g`, we might ask the question:

> Does the average body mass of a penguin differ by sex?

This question asks if the mean is different between two presumably independent samples of penguins (male and female penguins). 

Just like before, we can create tables and figures to create an overview of the variables. 

5. What should we type in the right hand side of the formula to get a summary table of body_mass_g by sex?
```{r}
#| label: Two sample t-test summary statistics
#| eval: false

favstats(body_mass_g ~ sex , data = penguins, na.rm = TRUE)
```


6. Try making a box plot to display the difference in body mass by sex.

```{r}
#| label: Box plot of body mass and sex

penguins |> 
  drop_na(sex) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = body_mass_g, fill = sex)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = "Body Mass (g)", 
       fill = "Sex",
       title = "Body Mass by Sex of Penguins") +
  theme_classic()
```

Now let's conduct a two sample t-test. 

```{r}
#| label: Two sample t test
#| echo: true

t.test(body_mass_g ~ sex, data = penguins)

```

7. What is the p-value? 4.794e-16

8. What is the 95% confidence interval? [-840.5783, -526.2453]

9. What are the sample means of body mass? For females it is 3862.273 and for males it is 4545.685.

10. What can we conclude about the difference in average penguin body mass by sex? The difference in average body mass by sex is statistically significant.  


:::


## Chi Squared Test

Contingency tables or two-way tables are one way of displaying the number of counts between two categorical variables.

While there are a variety of methods for analyzing categorical data - we will briefly cover the chi-squared test of independence. This statistical test is used when the inference of interest is whether the categories of one variable are independent of another. 

For example, if we ask the question:

> Does the number of penguins on an island differ by year?

As a note, conducting a chi-squared test in R can be a bit tedious however there are alternative packages (see: `psych`) that are perhaps better suited to more advanced or nuanced applications of the chi-squared test.

To conduct a chi-squared test:

First, create a `table()` of two categorical variables. Then, pipe the table output to the `chisq.test()` function. 

11. What variables do we need to specify in the `table` function? 

Type them inside the parenthesis using the `data_name$variable_name` format.

```{r}
#| label: chi sq table
table(penguins$year, penguins$island)
```


12. Try piping the table output to the `chisq.test()` function.

```{r}
#| label: chisq test

table(penguins$year, penguins$island) |> 
  chisq.test()

```


13. What conclusion can we make? Use the output to support your conclusion.

Year is not independent of island. 

## Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)

If we have a numeric variable that is grouped by or explained by a categorical variable, and we want to know whether the means differ between groups, we can conduct an ANOVA.

Once again, let us go back to the penguins data set. This time we will use island as an explanatory variable and bill depth as a response variable to answer the following question:

> Does the average bill depth length of penguins differ by island?

### Exploratory Data Analysis

First, we will make a table to provide descriptive statistics. 

```{r}
#| label: anova summary stats

# use favstats since bill_depth_mm is a numeric variable and we can easily get descriptive statistics. 
mosaic::favstats(~ bill_depth_mm, data = penguins) 

# we can use count() to get a table of frequencies for categorical variables like `island`. 
penguins |>
  count(island) 

# However, we can incorporate both numeric and categorical variables into favstats to get descriptive statistics of a numerical response variable grouped by a categorical variable. 
mosaic::favstats(bill_depth_mm ~ island, data = penguins) 
```

There are a couple of ways to visualize these descriptive findings. Feel free to explore and play around with different plot types. 

14. Create a figure, using `ggplot`, to visualize the results from the descriptive statistics table. 

```{r}
#| label: histogram for ANOVA
#| fig-cap: "Histograms displaying the variability of penguin bill depth between islands"
#| warning: false
#| message: false
ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_depth_mm, fill = island)) +
  geom_histogram() +
  facet_wrap(~ island, nrow = 3) +
  labs(title = "Bill Depth of Penguins by Island",
       fill = "Island",
       x = "Bill Depth (mm)",
       y = "Count") +
  theme_bw()

```

### Running ANOVA in R

When we are ready to conduct ANOVA to answer our research question, we can use the following code to do that:

```{r}
#| label: ANOVA code
aov(bill_depth_mm ~ island, data = penguins)
```


The immediate output is not incredibly informative. It does not contain information about the statistical significance of the ANOVA results. 

Often times it is more helpful to assign the model or statistical test as an object to our environment, and then use a function like `summary()` to get more detailed output. 

:::{.callout-tip}

The `<-` is used to assign code to an object. For example, `x <- 5 + 2` assigns the sum of 5 and 2 to an object, x. 

:::


```{r}
#| label: ANOVA summary

bill_anova <- aov(bill_depth_mm ~ island, data = penguins) # assign the ANOVA results to an object, bill_anova, to call upon later.

summary(bill_anova) # Extract more detailed results of the ANOVA including
```


15. What conclusion can you make about the different group means? At least one of the island's mean bill depth is different and statistically significant from the other islands.

If we want to identify which group mean is significantly different, we can use Tukey's Honest Significant Difference test to evaluate which pair of groups is different. This functions requires us to call up our ANOVA results we saved earlier:

```{r}
#| label: Tukey Test
TukeyHSD(bill_anova) # Run a Tukey test using the ANOVA results
```

16. Based on the TukeyHSD, which groups are significantly different? The Dream-Biscoe and Torgersen-Biscoe pairs are significantly different.

17. Which pair has the largest mean difference? The largest difference is in the Torgersen-Biscoe pair.

18. What is the 95% confidence interval for the Torgersen-Dream pair? The 95% CI is [-.515, .685].

## Linear Regression

Next we will introduce how to do correlation and simple linear regression in R, extract results from a regression model, using R markdown to write 

### Correlations

Conducting correlations using the base R function is similar to how we make a table. 


For example, a correlation between two variables this can be done doing the following:

```{r}
#| label: correlation with base R

stats::cor(penguins$bill_depth_mm, penguins$bill_length_mm, use = "complete.obs")

```

Notice that when we have `NA` values we need to tell the correlation function that we want it to use only the rows that have complete data. 

The `mosaic` package also has a function for running correlations that is set up similarly to the `favstats` function.

```{r}
#| label: correlation with mosaic

mosaic::cor(bill_depth_mm ~ bill_length_mm, data = penguins, use = "complete.obs")

```

Correlations for multiple variables is a bit more involved, but can be done!

```{r}
#| label: correlation matrix

penguins |> # specify data
  drop_na() |> # drop missing values for simplicity
  select(bill_depth_mm:body_mass_g) |> # selects only numerical variables
  cor() # pipes data into the correlation function

```


### Simple Linear Regression
If I want to evaluate how one variable is associated with another, I can conduct a linear regression. For example I might ask the question: 


> Do penguins with bigger bill depths have a large body mass?

Running a regression model is similar to the way we did ANOVA however this time we will use the `lm()` function. 

:::{.callout-note}
You may notice that the formula for `lm` includes a $1$ before the explanatory variable. This is a way of explicitly writing out an intercept term but is not necessary to successfully conduct regression.
:::

```{r}
#| label: Regression 

lm(body_mass_g ~ 1 + bill_depth_mm, data = penguins) 

```

```{r}
#| label: Regression Summary

fit1 <- lm(body_mass_g ~ 1 + bill_depth_mm, data = penguins) 
summary(fit1)
```


19. What are the intercept and slope values? The intercept is 7488.65 and the slope is -191.64.

20. What is the p-value for the slope? Less than 2e-16. 


### Evaluating Regression Assumptions

We can also plot the results of regression and create plots to assess the model assumptions.

For linear regression we assume:

a. Linear relationship between the response and explanatory variables.
b. Independence of observations.
c. Normally distributed residuals.
d. Equal variance of residuals.

There are several ways to look evaluate the regression assumptions of your model. However, we can easily evaluate the conditions for linear regression using the function `resid_panel` from the `ggResidpanel` package, which conveniently combines all the plots we can use to visually evaluate the regression assumptions. 

```{r}
#| label: Resid Panels
#| message: false
#| warning: false

library(ggResidpanel) # Need this package to use the resid_panel function
resid_panel(fit1)

```


### Multiple Linear Regression and Advanced Applications of Regression

This is a quick introduction and example of more complex applications of the `lm` function. We may cover this in more detail on Day 3.


Including multiple predictors to explain variability in the response or dependent variable

```{r}
#| label: Complex Regression Examples

# Using a subset of the data (i.e. filtering data used in analysis) 

lm(body_mass_g ~ 1 + bill_depth_mm, data = penguins, subset = year == 2007)


# Including multiple covariates

lm(body_mass_g ~ 1 + bill_depth_mm + flipper_length_mm + species, data = penguins)


# Interaction effects between explanatory variables

lm(body_mass_g ~ 1 +  bill_depth_mm  + flipper_length_mm + bill_depth_mm*species, data = penguins)


# Exponentiation (squaring, cubing, etc.) variables

lm(body_mass_g ~ 1 + bill_depth_mm^2, data = penguins)

# Log transforming variables

lm(body_mass_g ~ 1 + log(bill_depth_mm), data = penguins)
```


# Displaying Your Findings

After conducting your analysis, you want to share your incredible findings with the world. To maximize interpretability of your results here are some things we can do.

## Using R Markdown to write equations or Greek symbols.

While not essential, it can be helpful to know that you can include mathematical equations and Greek symbols in R Markdown by wrapping `$$` around LaTex code to display equations and symbols. 

For example, I can use:

```
$$
y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \epsilon
$$
```

to display a regression equation:

$$
y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \epsilon
$$
21. Try writing a regression equation for this model: 

`lm(body_mass_g ~ 1 + bill_depth_mm + flipper_length_mm + species, data = penguins)` 

$$
{Body \ Mass = \beta_0 + \beta_1 (Bill \ Depth) + \beta_2 (Flipper \ Length) + \beta_3(Species) 
$$
# Supplementary Information

## Creating Tables

Here are some ways we can make easy and simple tables of the regression output in R.

Using `knitr` and `kable`:

```{r}
#| label: Tables with Kable

fit1 |> 
  broom::tidy() |> 
  kable()

```

The `modelsummary` function will produce a comprehensive table of your model results. This probably has more information than you will need depending on your model. The help documentation contains information for modifying the table based on your needs.

```{r}
#| label: Model Summary Table
modelsummary(fit1)
```

### Displaying Multiple Models 

When you have multiple models that you want to display there are a couple more steps. First, we can assign multiple models into a list. Then, we can put the list of models into either function. 

```{r}
#| label: tbl-multiple_models
#| tbl-cap: "Example of a table displaying results from separate regression models"


# First, assign regression models to object
fit1 <- lm(body_mass_g ~ 1 + bill_depth_mm, data = penguins)

fit2 <- lm(body_mass_g ~ 1 + bill_depth_mm + flipper_length_mm + species, data = penguins)

fit3 <- lm(body_mass_g ~ 1 +  bill_depth_mm  + flipper_length_mm + bill_depth_mm*species, data = penguins)

# Combine objects into a list and assign it the name models 
models <- list(fit1, fit2, fit3) # 

# Call the object into the function modelsummary
modelsummary(models)
```


## Creating Figures and Plots with Regression Output

If you want to display the output from regression, you can use the `tidy()` function to create a tidy tibble of regression and plot them using `ggplot`. Using the models from @tbl-multiple_models as an example:

```{r}
#| label: Creating tidy output from regression data

# Create objects of tidy data from regression models, specify that we want confidence intervals
fit1_tidy <- tidy(fit1, conf.int = TRUE)
fit2_tidy <- tidy(fit2, conf.int = TRUE)
fit3_tidy <- tidy(fit3, conf.int = TRUE)

# Since the tidy data all contain the same number and names of columns, we can join them by binding their rows to make a complete data set. 
## The .id argument provides an id column to link each row to its original data set.

fit_plot <- bind_rows(fit1_tidy, fit2_tidy, fit3_tidy, .id = "fit")

```

Now that we have a tidy data set of our regression output, we can plot the regression coefficient along with error bars.

For example, I may want to see how the slope for `bill_depth_mm` differs across models.

```{r}
#| label: Table of bill depth across models

# Here I am filtering the data to show rows where my term is the slope of interest, then removing columns that I do not want to display in my table, lastly passing the transformed data frame to the kable function to make a table. 
fit_plot |> 
  filter(term == "bill_depth_mm") |> 
  select(-c(std.error, statistic, p.value)) |> 
  kable()
```


```{r}
#| label: fig-bill_depth_coefs


fit_plot |> 
  filter(term == "bill_depth_mm") |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = fit, y = estimate)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = conf.low, ymax = conf.high)) +
  labs(title = "Effect of Bill Depth (mm) on Body Mass (g) across Models",
       x = "Model",
       y = "Slope") +
  theme_bw()

```
